segments.s680-g25: مباشید is a negative imperative in classical Persian (2nd person plural) meaning 'do not be/do not remain'. It governs the predicative adjective ایمن ('safe'), so the clause literally says 'do not be safe'. بران رزمگاه is a locative phrase ('on/upon that battlefield') where بران is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'on that' and رزمگاه is the noun 'battlefield'.
segments.s680-g26: که here is a causal conjunction 'because/for', introducing the reason: خسرو (the name) is the subject. شبیخون is a noun meaning 'night-raid' used with the verb کند ('does/makes'); کند is the simple present used in classical narrative to express a future or imminent action ('will launch'). با سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'with (his) host/army'.
segments.s680-g27: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. The verb شنید ('heard') appears before the subject in this poetic order: 'پاسخ شنید' = 'heard the reply'. آن فرستاده مرد is the subject phrase 'that messenger-man' (مرد here = 'man' / 'the man'), identifying who heard it.
segments.s680-g28: سوی لشکر is a directional phrase 'toward the army'. پهلوان شد uses شد (past-tense 'became' / 'turned') with پهلوان ('hero, champion') as the predicative — poetically 'he became/turned into a hero' or 'he acted like a champion'. چو گرد is a simile 'like a whirl(dust)', where چو = 'like' and گرد here evokes dust/whirl, so the whole clause means he rushed toward the army 'like a whirlwind'. The verb-subject/order is compact and typical of epic Persian.
segments.s680-g29: همه is a universal determiner 'the whole/all'. لشکرآتش is a poetic compound referring to the host made fiery (literally 'army of fire' or the army's fires) and functions as the subject. برافروختند is past-tense plural 'they kindled/lit' (classical past), so together 'the whole host kindled fires' or 'the host became an army of fire by lighting torches'.
segments.s680-g30: بهر جای means 'at/for every place/post' (بهر = 'for/to/toward' used in a distributive locative sense). شمعی is an indefinite noun 'a candle' (here distributively: 'a candle at each place'). همی‌سوختند is an imperfective past (the classical progressive prefix همی plus سوختند) meaning 'they were burning' or 'burned', agreeing with the implied plural 'candles' — 'a candle burned at every post'.
segments.s680-g9: سپه ماند is a simple past clause: ماند (past of ماندن) = 'remained' and precedes or follows its subject in poetry; here سپه 'the host/army' is the subject. از + place names (از ... واردبیل) lists the area where they remained ('from Barda to Ardabil' or 'stretching from ... to ...'); از is the preposition 'from'.
segments.s680-g10: از ارمنیه نیز introduces another region with از 'from' and نیز 'also/likewise'. بی‌مرد وخیل is a nominal phrase meaning 'without men and troops' (بی‌- 'without' + مرد/خیل); in syntax it continues the locative/partitive idea — i.e. in those regions there were no men or forces remaining.
segments.s680-g11: ازیشان is a fused prepositional phrase (از ایشان) meaning 'of/among them' or 'as for them'. برزم here refers to the battlefield/field (an archaic or poetic usage) and is followed by اندرون 'within' and نیست 'is not' to state absence: 'there is no fear among them in the field.' باک is the noun 'fear/dread' that is negated by نیست.
segments.s680-g12: This is a correlative listing introduced by چه ... چه 'whether ... or ...' (each چه marks one alternative). مردان بردع names one group ('the men of Bard(a)/Bardā' — بردع is a place-name or group here), and چه یک مشت خاک gives the contrasting image 'or a single handful of earth' (مشت = 'handful'). The clause contrasts full men with mere handfuls to stress there is no fear either way.
segments.s680-g13: شنیدند is past tense, 3rd person plural 'they heard'. In this poetic order the verb appears before its subject گردنکشان ('the neck‑pullers' = rebels/proud ones) and the object is این سخن ('this speech/word'). So: 'the rebels heard this speech/word.'
segments.s680-g14: که introduces reported content 'that ...'. بهرام جنگ‌آور is a nominative phrase 'Bahram the warrior.' افگند is a past transitive verb 'threw/cast (down)' and takes بن as its object here (بن = 'root/foundation/base'), so the clause reads 'that Bahram the warrior cast down the (very) foundation.'
segments.s680-g15: گزیدند is past plural 'they chose'. زلشکر is a fused or contracted prepositional expression meaning 'from the army/host' (ز + لشکر); in context it marks the source ('they chose from the army'). مردی دبیر is the direct object: 'a man, a scribe' (مردی = 'a man'; دبیر = 'scribe/scholar').
segments.s680-g16: This is a string of appositive descriptors: سخن گوی 'one who speaks words' = 'eloquent speaker' (سخن + گوی), و 'and' links داننده 'knower/expert' with ویادگیر 'and memorizer/reciter' — these are nouns qualifying the chosen man. The order is noun + modifier(s), typical for a list of epithets.
segments.s680-g1: وزین روی بنشست بهرام گرد is a compact clause: وزین روی (lit. 'with a dignified face/countenance') is an adverbial phrase modifying the verb بنشست ('sat down', 3rd‑person past). بهرام گرد is the subject (Bahrām Gird — here گرد functions as part of the personal name/epithet). Word order is topic/adverbial → verb → subject, which is common in older Persian poetic style.
segments.s680-g2: بزرگان برفتند با او وخرد is a coordinated clause: بزرگان ('the great ones/elders') is the subject, برفتند is the verb (3rd‑person plural past 'they went/approached'), and با او وخرد ('with him and the wise' or 'with him and wisdom') is a prepositional/coordinate complement showing accompaniment. The conjunctional / copulative و is fused onto خرد here, listing 'the wise' along with 'بزرگان'.
segments.s680-g3: سپهبد بپرسید زان سرکشان: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (commander); بپرسید is a past‑tense narrative form 'asked'. زان here is a prepositional/relational particle 'about/from that' (i.e., 'about those'), and سرکشان is the plural noun ('the rebellious ones'/'rebels') — an older plural/agentive formation. The whole phrase means the commander asked concerning those rebels.
segments.s680-g4: که آمد زخویشان شما را نشان is a relative clause modifying 'سرکشان': که ('who/that') + آمد ('came') gives 'who came', زخویشان ('from their kinsmen' — زِ + خویشان) indicates origin/association, and شما را نشان ('a show/sign to you' or 'to show themselves to you') places شما as the definite object (را marks that object) and نشان is a noun 'sign/show'. The relative pronoun که introduces the subordinate clause.
segments.s680-g5: فرستید هرکس که دارید خویش is an imperative/send‑command clause: فرستید is a 2nd‑person (pl./polite) imperative or hortative 'send', هرکس که دارید 'whoever you have' is a free relative (هرکس = 'whoever'; که introduces the clause; دارید = 'you have' present), and خویش is the possessive/kinship element ('your own' / 'of kin') — here the possessive is split into خوی + ش in the list but functions together as 'your kin'.
segments.s680-g6: که باشند یکدل به گفتار وکیش is a purpose/result clause introduced by که; باشند is the 3rd‑person plural subjunctive/present 'may be' or 'be', indicating desired state, یکدل is an adjective 'single‑hearted/like‑minded', and به گفتار و کیش ('in speech and in faith') gives the domains of unity. The conjunction و links گفتار and کیش; the clause expresses the purpose or condition for sending them.
segments.s680-g7: گریشان بیایند وفرمان کنند begins with a conditional particle: گر(ی)شان = گر + ایشان 'if they', where گر is the classical conditional 'if'. بیایند is the (subjunctive/potential) 'they come', و + فرمان + کنند = 'and issue/command' (کنند is 3rd‑person plural subjunctive/optative 'let them do/let them issue'). Together this sets a protasis 'if they come and command'.
segments.s680-g8: به پیمان روان را گروگان کنند is the apodosis/result: به پیمان ('by/under the covenant' — به introduces the legal/ceremonial basis), روان را (روان as the object to be taken; را is the definite object marker) گروگان کنند ('let them make/take [him] hostage' — کنند is 3rd‑person plural subjunctive). Note that روان here functions as a proper/definite referent (hence object marking).
segments.s680-g17: بیامد is the simple past third-person singular 'he/she/it came'. گوی here names the appointed 'speaker' (poetic use; the input senses list does not give a perfect match, but it functions as 'the one who speaks'). با دلی پر ز راز is an adjectival/prepositional phrase: با + دلی ('with a heart') + پر ز ('full of') + راز ('secrets'). Word order is typical Persian: verb, subject/agent, then an adjunct describing his state.
segments.s680-g18: همی‌بود is an imperfect/continuous past meaning 'he continued to be / he kept on'. پویان is a participial/agentive form 'seeking, moving' describing the subject's activity. شب دیریاز is an adverbial time-phrase: شب 'night' qualified by دیریاز 'long (lasting)', so the whole clause means 'he continued seeking through the long night.'
segments.s680-g19: بگفت is past 'he said / he related' (the b- prefix is common in poetic pasts). آنچ is the relative 'that which / what'. بشنید is past 'he heard' and here functions as the verb of the subordinate content clause ('what he had heard'). زان ('from that / from them') precedes مهتران and marks the source; مهتران (poetic plural of مهتر) means 'elders/chiefs' (here rendered 'those chiefs').
segments.s680-g20: ازان is 'from those / from that source' and links to بشنید in the previous segment. نامداران و کنداوران are coordinated nouns ('the renowned ones and the diggers/excavators') specifying from whom he had heard the report; poetic diction allows this list-like apposition.
segments.s680-g21: از ایرانیان is a prepositional phrase 'from the Iranians' marking the source of the information. پاسخ is the direct object 'the reply'. ایدون here is a verbal form meaning 'to come / coming' and combines with شنید ('he heard') to mean 'he heard the coming reply' or more naturally 'he heard the reply (which said)'. شنید is simple past 'he heard'.
segments.s680-g22: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. تا is the temporal/subordinating 'until'. رزم لشکر is a genitive compound 'the army's battle' (i.e. 'the army going out to battle'). نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive form 'does not / should not come' and here means 'would not appear', and پدید (here at clause end) means 'appear / come forth' — together: 'that the army will not appear until it goes out to battle.'
segments.s680-g23: This line uses compact/poetic morphology: یکی = 'one / someone / if one' sets a conditional sense. مازخسرو is a contracted poetic sequence (ma- + z + خسرو) meaning roughly 'of/from Khosrow' or 'belonging to Khosrow' depending on context; here it indicates the party related to Khosrow. نگردیم is negative present/subjunctive/optative 'we do not turn / we do not return' and باز is the adverb 'again' or 'back'. Together the segment expresses the conditional 'if one of us does not return from Khosrow again.'
segments.s680-g24: بترسیم is first-person-plural present-subjunctive/optative 'we fear / let us fear' (the b- prefix appears in subjunctive forms in older/poetic Persian). کین here functions as a demonstrative/introductory particle meaning 'this/that' in poetic usage (sense not given in the provided list, so set to -1). کارگردد is a passive/intransitive verbal phrase 'the work/affair will be done / will take place', and دراز is the adjective 'long' — 'we fear that this affair will be prolonged.'

sentences.s680-s13: Do not feel safe on that battlefield, for Khosrow will make a night-raid with his host.
sentences.s680-s14: When that messenger heard the reply, he rushed toward the army like a whirlwind.
sentences.s680-s15: The whole host kindled fires; at every post a candle burned.
sentences.s680-s5: The host remained — from Barda to Ardabil; and from Armenia, too, there were no men or forces.
sentences.s680-s6: Among them there is no fear on the field — whether the men of Barda or but a handful of dust.
sentences.s680-s7: The rebels heard this word: that Bahram the warrior had cast down the foundation.
sentences.s680-s8: From the host they chose a man, a scribe — an eloquent speaker and a learned memorizer.
sentences.s680-s1: With a dignified countenance Bahram Gird sat down; the grandees and the wise accompanied him.
sentences.s680-s2: The spahbed inquired about those rebels who had come from their kinsmen to show themselves to you.
sentences.s680-s3: Send whoever of your own kin you have, so that they may be of one heart in word and in faith.
sentences.s680-s4: If they come and give orders, let them, by covenant, take Ravan as hostage.
sentences.s680-s9: The speaker came with a heart full of secrets; through the long night he kept seeking.
sentences.s680-s10: He related what he had heard from those chiefs, from those renowned men and the diggers.
sentences.s680-s11: From the Iranians he heard the reply that the army would not appear until it went out to battle.
sentences.s680-s12: If one of us does not return from Khosrow again, we fear that this affair will be prolonged.
